frameworks.name: linear/nonlinear neuronal pooling
frameworks.one_line: MT responses pooled linearly with trainable weights followed by fixed power-law nonlinearity

frameworks.name: selective readout of sensory neurons
frameworks.one_line: learning sharpens weighting toward neurons most sensitive to the trained stimulus

frameworks.name: ideal-observer / neurometric analysis
frameworks.one_line: single-neuron neurometric functions used to benchmark psychometric improvements

frameworks.name: perceptual learning
frameworks.one_line: long-lasting improvement in discriminating simple sensory stimuli

core_question: How do the threshold and slope of psychometric functions co-vary during perceptual learning, and what pooling scheme explains the divergence from neurometric functions?
key_assumptions: MT neurons carry sensory information used for direction discrimination
MT response properties themselves do not change with training
perceptual readout can be modelled as weighted pooling plus a fixed nonlinearity
manipulations: amount of training on a direction-discrimination task
comparison of psychometric vs neurometric thresholds and slopes
varying linear pooling weights in the model
key_findings: training lowered psychometric thresholds and slightly shallowed psychometric slopes
the threshold-slope relationship was much weaker in MT neurometric functions
MT response properties did not change with training
interpretations: learning changes linear pooling weights to read out the most sensitive MT neurons
a fixed power-law nonlinearity makes the psychometric slope shallower as uninformative neurons are dropped
